main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Contrastive teacher-student framework for novelty detection","Crafting auxiliary OOD set by identifying and distorting core features of ID samples using Grad-CAM-based saliency maps and hard transformations","Light augmentations (e.g., color jitter) to produce style-shifted samples","Task-based knowledge distillation: teacher (pre-trained encoder + binary classifier) and student (trained from scratch) with a novel objective function aligning/diverging outputs for ID/OOD samples","Using discrepancy between student and teacher outputs as OOD score at inference"],"problems":["Robust novelty detection under style shifts (minor distribution shifts in style features)","Reducing reliance on style features and focusing on core features for novelty detection","Improving ND performance when only ID samples are available for training and no metadata or OOD samples are accessible"],"datasets":["Autonomous driving datasets (e.g., images from Germany and Los Angeles streets)","Large medical imaging datasets","Waterbird dataset","Synthetic datasets (not named explicitly)","Real-world benchmarks (not named explicitly)"],"metrics":["AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)"],"results":[{"metric":"AUROC (robust)","value":"up to 12.7% improvement over baselines"},{"metric":"AUROC (standard)","value":"up to 6.7% improvement over baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a data-centric, meta-data free pipeline for robust novelty detection under style shifts.","Crafts auxiliary OOD samples by identifying and distorting core regions of ID samples, rather than applying hard augmentations to the entire image.","Introduces a task-based knowledge distillation strategy with a novel objective function for aligning/diverging student-teacher outputs for ID/OOD samples.","Provides a method that does not require environmental annotations or extra data from different environments, unlike prior works.","Demonstrates superior performance compared to nine recent ND methods, including knowledge distillation-based and other approaches."]}

methods: Contrastive teacher-student framework for novelty detection
Crafting auxiliary OOD set by identifying and distorting core features of ID samples using Grad-CAM-based saliency maps and hard transformations
Light augmentations (e.g., color jitter) to produce style-shifted samples
Task-based knowledge distillation: teacher (pre-trained encoder + binary classifier) and student (trained from scratch) with a novel objective function aligning/diverging outputs for ID/OOD samples
Using discrepancy between student and teacher outputs as OOD score at inference
problems: Robust novelty detection under style shifts (minor distribution shifts in style features)
Reducing reliance on style features and focusing on core features for novelty detection
Improving ND performance when only ID samples are available for training and no metadata or OOD samples are accessible
datasets: Autonomous driving datasets (e.g., images from Germany and Los Angeles streets)
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

results.value: up to 12.7% improvement over baselines
up to 6.7% improvement over baselines

novelty_claims: Proposes a data-centric, meta-data free pipeline for robust novelty detection under style shifts.
Crafts auxiliary OOD samples by identifying and distorting core regions of ID samples, rather than applying hard augmentations to the entire image.
Introduces a task-based knowledge distillation strategy with a novel objective function for aligning/diverging student-teacher outputs for ID/OOD samples.
Provides a method that does not require environmental annotations or extra data from different environments, unlike prior works.
Demonstrates superior performance compared to nine recent ND methods, including knowledge distillation-based and other approaches.

methods: AutoAugOOD: probabilistic auto-negative pair generation method for contrastive learning
Adaptive contrastive learning with dynamic, data-driven hard augmentations based on KL divergence in feature space
Comprehensive evaluation protocol considering both mean and standard deviation of performance across datasets
problems: Universality in novelty detection: generalization and transferability across distribution shifts in training and test data
Poor robustness and adaptability of existing novelty detection methods to new datasets and corrupted data
Lack of negative pairs in contrastive learning for novelty detection due to semantic similarity of inlier data
datasets: Corrupted versions of common benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10-C, MVTecAD-C)
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
Standard deviation of AUROC across datasets

results.metric: Average AUROC improvement across diverse benchmark datasets
results.value: 7% increase
50% reduction
5% improvement

novelty_claims: Proposes AutoAugOOD, a probabilistic method for automatically generating negative pairs in contrastive learning for novelty detection, using KL divergence and adaptive hard augmentations.
Introduces a universal novelty detection framework that generalizes across various distribution shifts in both training and test data, including corrupted and real-world scenarios.

Demonstrates significant improvements in both average performance and robustness (variance reduction) across multiple datasets and novelty detection settings (one-class, unlabeled multi-class, labeled multi-class).
Evaluates universality of novelty detection methods using both mean and standard deviation of performance across datasets, rather than single-dataset metrics.
Shows that adaptive, data-driven augmentations outperform fixed, manually selected augmentations for negative pair generation in novelty detection.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["OOD-aware Prototypical Supervised Contrastive Learning (OOD-aware SupCon)","Extension of Supervised Contrastive (SupCon) loss with two additional contrastive terms: (1) a term that pushes auxiliary OOD representations away from ID representations, (2) a term that pushes OOD features far from class prototypes while pulling ID features closer to their prototypes","Prototype-based classifier instead of softmax classifier","Feature mixing/augmentation techniques to generate pseudo-OOD features when auxiliary OOD data is unavailable"],"problems":["Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection for safe deployment of machine learning models","Overconfidence of classification models on OOD data when trained with Cross-Entropy loss","Improving OOD detection performance while maintaining or improving in-distribution (ID) classification (FPR)","value":"Significant reduction compared to previous methods (exact values not provided in excerpt)"},{"metric":"Classification Accuracy","value":"Maintained or improved compared to baseline methods (exact values not provided in excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes an OOD-aware supervised contrastive learning framework that explicitly incorporates OOD data (real or synthesized) into the contrastive loss.","Introduces two new loss terms: one to push OOD features away from ID features, and another to push OOD features away from class prototypes.","Uses a prototype-based classifier instead of a softmax classifier for improved OOD detection.","Presents a simple and efficient feature augmentation technique to generate OOD-like features when auxiliary OOD data is not available.","Demonstrates state-of-the-art OOD detection performance, especially in reducing false positive rate, while maintaining or improving ID classification accuracy."]}

methods: OOD-aware Prototypical Supervised Contrastive Learning (OOD-aware SupCon)

Extension of Supervised Contrastive (SupCon) loss with two additional contrastive terms: (1) a term that pushes auxiliary OOD representations away from ID representations, (2) a term that pushes OOD features far from class prototypes while pulling ID features closer to their prototypes
Prototype-based classifier instead of softmax classifier
Feature mixing/augmentation techniques to generate pseudo-OOD features when auxiliary OOD data is unavailable
problems: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection for safe deployment of machine learning models
Overconfidence of classification models on OOD data when trained with Cross-Entropy loss
Improving OOD detection performance while maintaining or improving in-distribution (ID) classification accuracy

results.value: Significant reduction compared to previous methods (exact values not provided in excerpt)
Maintained or improved compared to baseline methods (exact values not provided in excerpt)

novelty_claims: Proposes an OOD-aware supervised contrastive learning framework that explicitly incorporates OOD data (real or synthesized) into the contrastive loss.
Introduces two new loss terms: one to push OOD features away from ID features, and another to push OOD features away from class prototypes.
Uses a prototype-based classifier instead of a softmax classifier for improved OOD detection.
Presents a simple and efficient feature augmentation technique to generate OOD-like features when auxiliary OOD data is not available.
Demonstrates state-of-the-art OOD detection performance, especially in reducing false positive rate, while maintaining or improving ID classification accuracy.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Outlier Aware Learning (OAL) framework: synthesizes OOD training data in the latent space using Stable Diffusion","Mutual Information-based Contrastive Learning module (MICL): regularizes the model's decision boundary by amplifying the distinction between ID and OOD features","In-Distribution Knowledge Distillation (IDKD): knowledge distillation module to prevent degradation of ID classification accuracy when training with OOD data","k-Nearest Neighbor (k-NN) sampling: to sample outliers from the penultimate layer of a vision model as token conditions for Stable Diffusion"],"problems":["Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection: identifying unknown samples or objects in real-world deployments","How to collect representative OOD samples for outlier exposure training","How to regularize the model effectively using generated OOD data","Preventing degradation of in-distribution (ID) classification accuracy when training with OOD data"],"datasets":["CIFAR-10","CIFAR-100"],"metrics":["AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)","In-distribution (ID) classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"AUROC (CIFAR-100, compared to Dream-OOD)","value":"OAL outperforms Dream-OOD using only a very small batch of OOD data"},{"metric":"ID classification accuracy","value":"Maintained or improved compared to effective and efficient network training with fewer synthesized outliers.","Introduces a mutual information-based contrastive learning module (MICL) to better regularize the model's decision boundary methods: Outlier Aware Learning (OAL) framework: synthesizes OOD training data in the latent space using Stable Diffusion
Mutual Information-based Contrastive Learning module (MICL): regularizes the model's decision boundary by amplifying the distinction between ID and OOD features
In-Distribution Knowledge Distillation (IDKD): knowledge distillation module to prevent degradation of ID classification accuracy when training with OOD data
k-Nearest Neighbor (k-NN) sampling: to sample outliers from the penultimate layer of a vision model as token conditions for Stable Diffusion
problems: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection: identifying unknown samples or objects in real-world deployments
How to collect representative OOD samples for outlier exposure training
How to regularize the model effectively using generated OOD data
Preventing degradation of in-distribution (ID) classification accuracy when training with OOD data
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

results.value: OAL outperforms Dream-OOD using only a very small batch of OOD data
Maintained or improved compared to prior outlier synthesis approaches (e.g., Dream-OOD, VOS)

novelty_claims: Proposes generating OOD data in the latent space of Stable Diffusion, rather than the pixel space, facilitating more effective and efficient network training with fewer synthesized outliers.
Introduces a mutual information-based contrastive learning module (MICL) to better regularize the model's decision boundary between ID and OOD data.
Develops a knowledge distillation module (IDKD) to boost ID classification performance while enhancing OOD detection.
Demonstrates superior OOD detection performance on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 benchmarks compared to both post hoc and outlier synthesis-based methods.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["VOS (Virtual Outlier Synthesis): synthesizing virtual outliers by sampling from low-likelihood regions of the class-conditional distribution in feature space","Unknown-aware training objective: contrastively shaping the uncertainty surface between in-distribution (ID) data and synthesized outliers","Simultaneous training for ID task (classification or object detection) and OOD uncertainty regularization"],"problems":["Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for neural networks","Overconfident predictions on OOD data due to lack of unknown supervision during training","Difficulty and impracticality of obtaining real outlier datasets for model regularization","OOD detection in object detection models (not just image classification)"],"datasets":["Common OOD detection benchmarks (not specified by name in the provided content)","Object detection datasets (not specified by name in the provided content)"],"metrics":["FPR95 (False Positive Rate at 95% True Positive Rate)","Accuracy (on in-distribution task)"],"results":[{"metric":"FPR95 reduction (object detection)","value":"up to 9.36% lower than previous best method"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes VOS, a general framework for OOD detection that adaptively synthesizes virtual outliers in feature space, rather than relying on real outlier datasets or pixel-space synthesis.","Introduces a novel unknown-aware training objective that contrastively shapes the uncertainty between ID and synthesized OOD data.","Demonstrates that VOS is effective for both object detection and image classification, bridging a gap as prior work focused mainly on classification.","Shows that VOS achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging object detection OOD tasks, with significant improvements in FPR95.","Provides extensive ablation studies contrasting different outlier synthesis approaches, showing the advantages of feature-space synthesis over GAN-based or noise-based outlier generation."]}

methods: VOS (Virtual Outlier Synthesis): synthesizing virtual outliers by sampling from low-likelihood regions of the class-conditional distribution in feature space
Unknown-aware training objective: contrastively shaping the uncertainty surface between in-distribution (ID) data and synthesized outliers
Simultaneous training for ID task (classification or object detection) and OOD uncertainty regularization
problems: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection for neural networks
Overconfident predictions on OOD data due to lack of unknown supervision during training

results.value: up to 9.36% lower than previous best method

novelty_claims: Proposes VOS, a general framework for OOD detection that adaptively synthesizes virtual outliers in feature space, rather than relying on real outlier datasets or pixel-space synthesis.
Introduces a novel unknown-aware training objective that contrastively shapes the uncertainty between ID and synthesized OOD data.
Demonstrates that VOS is effective for both object detection and image classification, bridging a gap as prior work focused mainly on classification.
Shows that VOS achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging object detection OOD tasks, with significant improvements in FPR95.
Provides extensive ablation studies contrasting different outlier synthesis approaches, showing the advantages of feature-space synthesis over GAN-based or noise-based outlier generation.

methods: Normalization Perturbation (NP): Perturbing feature channel statistics in shallow CNN layers during training to synthesize diverse latent domain styles from a single source domain
Exploration of style-sensitive channels for effective domain style synthesis
problems: Domain generalization under real-world domain shifts, especially for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving
Overfitting of deep models to source domain style, leading to poor generalization on unseen target domains
Inefficiency and limitations of existing domain synthesis methods that require multiple source domains or are computationally expensive

results.value: Reduced distance between source and target domain feature distributions after applying NP
Model trained with NP can effectively blend distinct domain style distributions and generalizes better on target domain

novelty_claims: Proposes Normalization Perturbation (NP), a simple and effective method that perturbs feature channel statistics in shallow CNN layers to synthesize diverse domain styles from a single source domain
NP does not require any changes to model architecture, extra input, learnable parameters, or loss functions
NP is extremely easy to implement and generalizes well under various real-world domain shifts, outperforming previous DG and UDA methods on multiple dense prediction tasks
First to identify and address the biased feature distribution in low-level layers as the main cause of domain overfitting in real-world domain shifts

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Model-Specific Out-of-Distribution (MS-OOD) Detection framework","Evaluation of OOD detection methods: Maximum Softmax Probabilities (MSP), Energy Score, Maximum Logit Score (MLS), Virtual-logit Matching (ViM), GradNorm"],"problems":["Unified out-of-distribution (OOD) detection considering both semantic shift and covariate shift","Detecting test examples that a deployed model is unable to predict correctly (model-specific rejection)","Limitations of conventional OOD detection focusing only on semantic shift"],"datasets":["ImageNet","ImageNetV2","ImageNet-Sketch"],"metrics":["Detection accuracy","True positive rate","Softmax probability scores"],"results":[{"metric":"Detection of misclassified ID and C-OOD examples (MSP method)","value":"MSP performs favorably, often outperforming more advanced methods (quantitative values not specified in provided text)"},{"metric":"Detection of S-OOD examples (robust classifier)","value":"Stronger classifiers make S-OOD detection easier, but exceptions exist depending on method and classifier (quantitative values not specified)"},{"metric":"Score separation (softmax probabilities)","value":"Misclassified ID examples have lower scores than correctly classified ID examples (quantitative values not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel, unifying framework (MS-OOD Detection) for OOD detection from a model-specific perspective, unifying semantic and covariate shift scenarios.","Defines OOD detection in terms of whether a deployed classifier can correctly classify an example, rather than only distributional shift.","Provides a comprehensive empirical study across multiple models, datasets, and OOD detection methods, revealing new and unified insights.","Shows that the best OOD detection method is model-specific and varies with the type of OOD example and classifier robustness."]}

methods: Model-Specific Out-of-Distribution (MS-OOD) Detection framework
Evaluation of OOD detection methods: Maximum Softmax Probabilities (MSP), Energy Score, Maximum Logit Score (MLS), Virtual-logit Matching (ViM), GradNorm
problems: Unified out-of-distribution (OOD) detection considering both semantic shift and covariate shift
Detecting test examples that a deployed model is unable to predict correctly (model-specific rejection)
Limitations of conventional OOD detection focusing only on semantic shift

results.metric: Detection of misclassified ID and C-OOD examples (MSP method)
results.value: MSP performs favorably, often outperforming more advanced methods (quantitative values not specified in provided text)
Stronger classifiers make S-OOD detection easier, but exceptions exist depending on method and classifier (quantitative values not specified)
Misclassified ID examples have lower scores than correctly classified ID examples (quantitative values not specified)

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel, unifying framework (MS-OOD Detection) for OOD detection from a model-specific perspective, unifying semantic and covariate shift scenarios.
Defines OOD detection in terms of whether a deployed classifier can correctly classify an example, rather than only distributional shift.
Provides a comprehensive empirical study across multiple models, datasets, and OOD detection methods, revealing new and unified insights.
Shows that the best OOD detection method is model-specific and varies with the type of OOD example and classifier robustness.
